# 

# 系统设计真题分享 \- Ads Aggregation / Top K problem

# Important concepts

- Basic understanding of the ads system  
- Count-min sketch  
- Slow and fast path  
- Downsampling

# Another pre-read: context of ads industry

Imagine we are Google and there are advertisers (say, Temu) who want to place their ads on Google. The ads can appear either above or below search results, or in places such as Google Map, or Gmail. 

As an advertiser, I may have 10 different ads (for example, videos of different lengths, images with different sizes, different images for different groups of customers), and I care about how many times each ad was viewed/clicked by customers and I want to maximize the reach of customers within my budget.   
I also care which 5 out of the 10 ads I have performed the best, this way I know the trend of customers and can replace my low performing ads. 

Google also cares about the clicks of each ad every day (or every hour), because I need to make sure I don’t overspend the customer’s budget (ads are usually charged by every 1000 views). At the same time, I also want to spend every penny of the customer’s budget because I bill the customer based on these events.

# Requirement

**Design an ads reporting system that can answer two questions**:

- Counter of clicks for a particular ad in past X minutes  
- Top K most clicked ads in past X minutes

**✍️Note**: Both K and X are arbitrary numbers, they can be small as top 5 in the past 1 minute or large as top 100 in the past 24 hours. ASK interviewer if they have a desired access pattern. Otherwise, propose to make these two parameters configurable. Also it’s reasonable to propose a low bound for X to be 1 minute window.

**Do we need to design the event consumption module?**

Some companies actually want to discuss how the ad events were ingested into our system. However we are not designing an ads event collection system. For this problem, we assume there is a data stream provided already.

The data stream contains raw events of ad clicks/views:

{event\_id, user\_id, ad\_id, view/click, timestamp, ip, location, device….}

**✍️Note**: **DRIVE** conversations, make reasonable assumptions of the data stream. Write down event data model. 

**Do we need to support filtering by metadata (such as location, user device)?** 

This is most likely a global scale problem. In that case, it would be important to answer the top K question by region (US, Europe… etc). 

Advertisers may also feel interested in the number of clicks by devices (web, ios, Android) because they typically map to very different audiences. 

For this problem, we add filtering by metadata to our stretch goal.

## Edge case

#### Delayed events

Delayed events can affect top K results since this is a sliding window problem. 

#### Duplicated events

The data stream can contain duplicate events, which means we need to handle idempotency updates by event\_id if this is a common issue.

#### Disaster recover and replay events

For whatever reason, the data stream may have a subset of events replayed and duplicated. It should be as transparent to the external user as possible.

## Non functional Requirements

#### Scalability

1Billion ads events per day \~= 10000 QPS, PEAK 50000 QPS. 1B \* 100byte \~= 100GB raw data per day. 

100M ads placed, assume we store a counter per ads per minute. 100M \* 1440 \* 8 byte \~= 1TB data per day. 

#### Availability

No single point of failure, tolerates network/hardware failures. Data should be available in such failures.

#### Latency

Getting top K should be fast (\<200ms p99 latency).   
Getting counter for an ad should also be fast (\~50ms p99 latency)

#### Accuracy

We want to be as accurate as we can get. The tradeoff is that the higher accuracy we want, the less available the system will be. Imagine we want 100% accuracy, we basically need to wait for all events to arrive and count through all events to get the answer. 

**✍️Note:** we will get to the trade of accuracy and other requirements later in our detail design. Just throw out some edge case scenarios.

# API Design

POST /count?ad\_id={AD\_ID}\&start\_time={}\&end\_time={}\&filter={}  
Response: {count: 123}

**✍️Note**: the minimum time bucket is 1 minute, so the start and end time timestamp represents the minute inclusively. For example: start time can be 2024-01-01-11-59-00, end time can be 2024-01-01-12-01-00 (inclusive)

POST /topK?k=123&=time\_window={1m|5m|1h|1d}\&filter={}  
Response: \[{ad\_id, count}...\]

**✍️Note:** I used a custom template to represent time window. E.g 1m \= data in the past 1 minute, 5m \= data in the past 5 minutes. This API will return an error if time\_window is not a supported format. This way I have more control over the data model in our storage. But if we need to support random time window (such as top K in the past 73 minutes), we will need to fine tune our data model

**✍️Note2**: filter can be a POST request body or can be a URL encoded string. We return nothing if the filter is not matched with any data.

# High level Design

INSERT HIGH LEVEL FLOW CHART HERE.

### DB

Ad Event DB: storing raw events.  
Ad Event Counter DB: storing counter for each ad for a time window. 

### Event Queue Handler

Consume events from the queue and maintain a counter for each ad.  
Precalculates top K most clicked events per minute in memory. 

### Offline downsampling service

Periodically downsampling event counter entries to larger time buckets. See detailed design below.

### API Server

Depending on the time window provided in the request, it either talks to the handler to get the pre-calculated data in memory, or directly talks to DB for persisted results. 

![][image1]

# Detail Technical Design

## Fast path

![][image2]  
We have multiple handlers consuming the queue. Each handler maintains two data structures in memory:

- A max heap of size K  
- A count min sketch of size 1000 \* 8

Both data structures hold data for 1 minute time window, and each handler stores 60 copies of these two data structures, so it has the full data of one hour window.

App server talks directly to these handlers to get top K or a counter for one ad. 

When an event comes, the handler updates the frequency for the ad\_id, and pushes to the min heap if the top of the heap is smaller than the new count.

**❓Why do we need a fast path**

The brutal-force way to get top K for the past minute is to filter all events in event DB, group by ad\_id, and then sort to get top K. However this is very expensive to do on each request (3M events in one minute window).  Therefore we need to pre-calculate the result for each time bucket. This way getting counter of 

**❓How much memory do we need for CMS?**

One CMS table can comfortably handle 100 million unique items. The size of the table determines the error rate (collision rate). Below is how we calculate the size of the table:

![][image3]  
For this problem, we can use a 2000 \* 8 size table, each field in the table stores a 4 byte unsigned integer, which is in total 64kb. 60 CMS tables can still fit into one instance’s memory easily. 

**❓How do we get top K in past 1 minute and how to get counter for an ad**

Even though one handler can handle 100 million unique items, we need more handlers because we need to handle 50k events per second (even though updating CMS in memory should be very fast). Assume each handler handles 5k events per second comfortably, we will need to have 10 handlers. 

**We can partition the queue by ad\_id, and have one handler handling one partition**. The app server will talk to all handlers and get local topKs, and merge sorted arrays to get the global top K.

Since we partition the handler by ad\_id, it is very straightforward to get the counter for an ad. The app server locates the correct handler by hashing the ad\_id, and can get a counter for that ad\_id during that time window in constant time. 

**❓How do we get top K in the past 5 minutes and how to get the total count for an ad**

![][image4]  
Because we only maintain a CMS table and a max heap for 1 minute bucket, getting the total count in the past  5 minutes is simple, we just sum up the counter in these 5 buckets. We also need to merge past 5 buckets to get the top K from the past 5 minutes. 

**✍️Note**: **It’s important to note that we can not simply merge top K from these 5 buckets to get global top K**. Why? Imagine in one bucket, the k+1 most clicked item has more counts than the sum of all other most clicked buckets, the k+1 item may end up being the top 5 of the past 5 minutes.

In the below example, D is actually the top 1 most clicked item. Even though D is out of top 3 in the first bucket.

![][image5]  
We handle this by storing more items in the max heap. For example, we have an upper bound of K being 100 (we at most query top 100 most clicked ads), then we will store a max heap of size 5K or even 10K for each bucket. The more we store in the max heap, the less likely we run into this issue.

**❓Availability: What if one handler goes down**

The handler is stateful and maintains 5 buckets (one for each minute), each bucket has a heap and a CMS table. We can persist the in-memory structure whenever we are starting a new bucket onto a file system. This way if one handler goes down, the new instance can load previous buckets. 

## Slow path

![][image6]  
In the slow path, we first re-partition all raw events onto a queue partitioned by ad\_id (this queue can be reused with the fast path above). And then we have lambda functions aggregating for each ad for a larger time window (e.g. 5 minutes), and writing each counter to the file system. 

We will have a map reduce job that runs continuously to scan for new counter data, and generates aggregated counters for larger time window. **This process is also called downsampling**.

The counters are written to an event counter DB for fast retrieval. 

**❓Why do we need a slow path**

The problem of fast path is that it’s not very accurate (in our design, the CMS table has 0.1% error rate). Also the more data we hold in memory, the less error resilient the system is. Another thing is if we need to query for a very long time window, aggregating over many time buckets becomes slow. Therefore, for data older than 1 hour, we can recalculate both the top K and counter for each ad and persist to DB. 

**❓Why do we need to re-partition the queue?**

In the real world, the original data stream is often randomly partitioned (partitioned by event\_id) to handle large throughput. We want to make sure events for one ad are handled by one lambda function so that we can aggregate all events in one instance. 

**❓How do we support different time window query**

The API server handles the logic to parse time window query.

For example: if query \== “1d”, we can either directly query a pre-calculated result for the past day, or aggregate over 24 1 hour window results. We can optimize the map reduce job to pre-calculate common queries. If query \== “4h”, aggregate over 4 1 hour window results.

**❓Availability**

Map reduce runner usually handles job failure and retry internally. Since this is an offline process, we can build a mechanism to verify outputs at the end of the map reduce job.

**✍️Note:** I am not going to dive deep into the map reduce low level details here. But if you are interviewing for a ML infra sort of position, dive deeper here\!  

## Combining slow and fast path

When the app server needs to query for data in the past 60 minutes, it talks to the fast path handlers. Data will be fresh but may have some errors.

If the app server needs to query for data \> past 1hr, it will start to query event counter DB. Data will be accurate, but may not contain the latest data yet. 

## Handle advanced filtering queries

In the fast path, we can add more handlers to track heap and counter by specific tags.  
In the slow path, map reduce jobs will generate more precalculated results for each time bucket.

## Database Design

#### Raw event DB

We have very high write throughput (50000 writes per second at peak) and it’s appending only, we don’t expect any row updates. In this case, we can use noSQL such as Cassandra, which is linearly scalable by adding more nodes.

Partition key: event\_id, sorting clustering key: timestamp. 

✍️Note: Cassandra does not check on writes so to make sure uniqueness by event\_id, we use it as the partition key. Timestamp as the clustering key helps with time range queries for the downsampling service.  

#### Event Counter DB

We have 100M ads, and we recalculate the counter every hour, which is 100M rows every hour. Only in disaster recovery situations do we need to recalculate and update by key. Therefore we can use a persisted scalable key-value store (such as cassandra).

**Counter table:**

Partition key: (ad\_id), sorting key: (window\_start\_time, aggregated\_window), column: counter

For example, (ad\_1, 20240101:10:00, hour) represents the counter of clicks for ad\_1 in the hour starting from  20240101:10:00. We can also represent a larger time bucket this way (ad\_1, 20240101:12:00, day).

Since all ads have the same cardinality (1 record per hour), we don’t need to worry about hot partitions unless we know users query for one particular ad most of the time, in that case, we can introduce a cache for this key. 

**Top K table:**

Partition key: (window\_start\_time, aggregated\_window), column: \[{ad\_id, counter}\] 

#### File system

As an intermediate step of the slow path, we need to store a pre-calculated counter for each ad and 5 minute window. This data never changes once written, and is only used for batch jobs so we can use a distributed file system such as S3.

File path: /year/month/day/time/block\_1.tmp  
Block file content: ad\_id1, 100, ad\_2, 200 etc.

# Other considerations

## Monitoring

This is a very complex system and requires monitoring on a few critical points:

- Number of events consumed on the fast path for each partition. If consumption is delayed on one handler, top K will not be accurate.  
- Handler memory metrics, all the handlers are stateful instances, so we need to make sure no memory leaks etc.  
- Map reduce jobs should finish each job within an hour otherwise we will not have data for the past hour. It’s critical to monitor reducer metrics (failure/retry rate).

# After-read

This problem can apply to other similar questions: 100 most searched keywords, 100 most viewed videos, 100 most liked photos on Instagram etc.
